Which of the following statements about this patient's conduction abnormality is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The constant PR intervals in the conducted beats confirm that this is definitively Mobitz type II second-degree block

choices.label: B
choices.text: The Mobitz classification cannot be applied because consecutively conducted beats are needed to distinguish type I from type II

choices.label: C
choices.text: The presence of a relationship between P waves and every other QRS confirms that this is complete atrioventricular block

choices.label: D
choices.text: The 2:1 conduction ratio indicates that this block is physiologic and related to increased parasympathetic tone at rest

choices.label: E
choices.text: The normal blood chemistry rules out cardiac disease as a cause, suggesting this is a medication-related conduction delay

explanation.summary: In 2:1 AV block, the Mobitz classification cannot be applied because only every other beat is conducted, so there are no consecutively conducted beats to assess PR interval behavior.
explanation.whyCorrect: To apply the Mobitz classification, one must observe the PR interval in consecutively conducted beats leading up to the dropped beat. In 2:1 block, only every other beat is conducted, so there are no consecutively conducted beats. The block could be either Mobitz type I or type II.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Although the PR intervals may appear constant, this criterion cannot be applied for Mobitz classification when the block is 2:1, since consecutively conducted beats are required to distinguish the types.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Complete heart block requires AV dissociation with no relationship between P waves and QRS complexes. The fact that every other P wave is followed by a QRS maintains a relationship, ruling out complete block.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A symptomatic 2:1 block with a dangerously slow rate of 43 bpm in a patient with no reversible cause is not physiologic and likely represents intrinsic cardiac disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The patient is on no medications, and normal blood chemistry does not rule out intrinsic cardiac disease such as fibrosis or degenerative conduction system disease.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with a history of chronic heart failure and left bundle branch block presents with worsening dyspnea on exertion. His ejection fraction has declined from 40% to 25% over the past year despite optimal medical therapy. Which of the following treatment strategies most directly addresses the pathophysiologic consequences of his conduction abnormality?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Implantation of a single-chamber ventricular pacemaker to increase the resting heart rate above 60 beats per minute

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiation of a beta-blocker to reduce heart rate and decrease myocardial oxygen demand during systolic contraction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cardiac resynchronization therapy with simultaneous pacing of the right and left ventricles to restore synchrony

choices.label: D
choices.text: Catheter ablation of the left bundle branch to eliminate the conduction delay and restore normal QRS duration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Administration of intravenous atropine to improve conduction velocity through the left bundle branch fibers

explanation.summary: Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) with biventricular pacing directly addresses the dyssynchronous ventricular activation caused by LBBB in patients with heart failure.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that patients with bundle branch block and heart failure may require resynchronization therapy with simultaneous pacemakers in the RV and LV. CRT restores synchronous ventricular activation, improving ejection fraction and symptoms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A single-chamber ventricular pacemaker would not address the dyssynchrony between right and left ventricles. Biventricular pacing is needed to restore coordinated contraction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While beta-blockers are part of heart failure therapy, they do not address the mechanical dyssynchrony caused by LBBB, which is the specific pathophysiologic consequence being targeted.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Catheter ablation of the left bundle branch would worsen, not improve, the conduction abnormality. Ablation destroys tissue and would not restore normal conduction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atropine works by blocking vagal input at the AV node and would not improve conduction through the bundle branches, which is the site of the block in LBBB.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with a history of anterior wall myocardial infarction is admitted to the coronary care unit. His initial ECG shows right bundle branch block. Over the next 12 hours, he develops intermittent dropped QRS complexes with constant PR intervals in the conducted beats. His cardiologist expresses concern about a potentially dangerous progression. Which of the following complications is the cardiologist most worried about?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Development of complete heart block due to the contralateral left bundle branch also becoming blocked

choices.label: B
choices.text: Development of atrial fibrillation due to atrial remodeling caused by the prolonged QRS duration on ECG

choices.label: C
choices.text: Development of Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome from new accessory pathway formation after the infarction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Development of sinoatrial node dysfunction due to ischemic damage extending to the right atrial tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: Development of first-degree AV block with asymptomatic PR prolongation that will require lifelong monitoring

explanation.summary: When one bundle branch is blocked and Mobitz type II block develops, the concern is that the contralateral branch may also become blocked, causing complete heart block.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material warns that if the contralateral bundle branch becomes blocked in a patient with existing bundle branch block, a very dangerous form of AV block may suddenly result. Mobitz type II with existing RBBB suggests the left bundle is also affected, and complete bilateral bundle branch block would cause third-degree AV block.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Atrial fibrillation is not a consequence of bundle branch block. The RBBB does not cause atrial remodeling leading to atrial fibrillation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: WPW is a congenital condition resulting from incomplete formation of AV valve ring insulators; it does not develop after myocardial infarction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While SA node dysfunction is possible with right coronary artery disease, the cardiologist's concern with progressive conduction disease in both bundle branches is complete heart block.
whyWrongByChoice.E: First-degree AV block is a benign condition. The concern here is far more serious: progression to complete heart block from bilateral bundle branch block, which could cause cardiac arrest.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with lightheadedness and near-syncope. Her heart rate is 38 beats/min. ECG shows grouped beating with progressive prolongation of the PR interval before each dropped QRS complex. She is hemodynamically symptomatic with blood pressure of 82/54 mmHg. She recently started verapamil for hypertension. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Implantation of a permanent pacemaker because this type of block is always chronic and irreversible by definition

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administration of intravenous verapamil to further slow AV conduction and allow the ventricles more filling time

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administration of intravenous atropine to block vagal input and improve conduction through the AV node acutely

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initiation of a beta-blocker to reduce the heart rate and prevent the premature beats that trigger the dropped QRS

choices.label: E
choices.text: Electrical cardioversion to terminate the arrhythmia and restore normal sinus rhythm with 1:1 AV conduction

explanation.summary: Symptomatic Mobitz type I block is treated acutely with intravenous atropine, which blocks vagal (muscarinic) input to improve AV nodal conduction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) block occurs at the AV node and is often dependent on vagal tone or certain drugs (like verapamil). When symptomatic, atropine is usually effective in the short term due to its muscarinic blocking (anti-vagal) properties, serving to buy time until the offending agent is removed or the process resolves.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mobitz type I, in contrast to type II, tends to occur in acute situations, be dependent on vagal tone or certain drugs, and be reversible. Permanent pacing is needed only in exceptional chronic cases.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Verapamil is a nondihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker that slows AV conduction and is likely the cause of this patient's block. Giving more verapamil would worsen the condition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-blockers slow AV conduction and heart rate, which would worsen the block and the hemodynamic compromise. They are a cause of AV block, not a treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cardioversion is used for tachyarrhythmias, not for bradyarrhythmias like second-degree AV block. The problem here is insufficient conduction, not an abnormal rapid rhythm.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man undergoes an ECG as part of a routine employment physical. The tracing shows a regular sinus rhythm at 72 beats/min with a PR interval of 180 milliseconds, a QRS duration of 88 milliseconds, and normal ST-T wave morphology. He has no cardiac symptoms or history. His physician tells him the ECG is normal. If this patient's AV node were to suddenly fail completely, which of the following intrinsic rates would be expected from the next available rescue pacemaker?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 60 to 80 beats per minute from the sinoatrial node taking over as the primary cardiac pacemaker

choices.label: B
choices.text: 40 to 50 beats per minute from atrial tissue serving as the next subsidiary pacemaker in the hierarchy

choices.label: C
choices.text: 30 to 40 beats per minute from the AV junction generating an escape rhythm below the level of block

choices.label: D
choices.text: 15 to 30 beats per minute from ventricular myocardium serving as the lowest rescue pacemaker available

choices.label: E
choices.text: 100 to 120 beats per minute from enhanced sympathetic stimulation compensating for the loss of AV conduction

explanation.summary: If the AV node fails completely, the next rescue pacemaker in the conduction hierarchy is the AV junction (distal to the block), with an intrinsic rate of 30-40 bpm.
explanation.whyCorrect: The intrinsic pacemaker rates are: SA node 60-80 bpm, atria 40-50 bpm, AV junction 30-40 bpm, and ventricles 15-30 bpm. If the AV node itself fails, the rescue pacemaker from the junctional tissue distal to the block (His bundle region) would fire at 30-40 bpm.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The SA node is already the primary pacemaker and would continue to fire, but its impulses cannot reach the ventricles through the failed AV node. The SA node rate is irrelevant to what drives the ventricles.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Atrial tissue (40-50 bpm) is above the level of the AV node block. Like the SA node, atrial impulses would not be able to cross the blocked AV node to reach the ventricles.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ventricular myocardium (15-30 bpm) is the lowest rescue pacemaker, but the AV junction at 30-40 bpm would be expected to take over first, as it is the next available pacemaker below the block.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While sympathetic stimulation may occur as a compensatory response, the intrinsic rate of the rescue pacemaker is determined by the tissue's automaticity, not by the sympathetic nervous system response.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with a history of Lyme disease presents with fatigue and dyspnea. Her heart rate is 34 beats/min. ECG shows P waves at a rate of 76/min marching through the QRS complexes with no consistent relationship. The QRS complexes are narrow at 90 milliseconds with a regular rate. Which of the following conclusions about the level of the block is best supported by the QRS morphology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The narrow QRS complexes indicate the block is at or above the bundle of His with a junctional escape rhythm

choices.label: B
choices.text: The narrow QRS complexes indicate the block is below the bundle of His with a ventricular escape rhythm

choices.label: C
choices.text: The narrow QRS complexes indicate the block is within the bilateral bundle branches at an infra-Hisian level

choices.label: D
choices.text: The narrow QRS complexes indicate the block is at the sinoatrial node with failure of impulse generation

choices.label: E
choices.text: The QRS morphology cannot provide any information about the anatomic level of the conduction block

explanation.summary: In complete heart block, narrow escape QRS complexes indicate the block is at or above the bundle of His, with a junctional escape pacemaker generating the ventricular rhythm.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that blockade at or above the bundle of His may produce narrow escape QRS complexes (<3 small boxes), while infra-Hisian blockade always produces wide escape QRS complexes. The narrow QRS (90 ms) indicates the escape rhythm originates at the His bundle or AV junction level.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Infra-Hisian block with a ventricular escape rhythm would produce wide QRS complexes, not the narrow 90 ms complexes seen in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bilateral bundle branch block is an infra-Hisian location that always produces wide escape complexes, inconsistent with the narrow QRS described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: SA node failure would not show P waves at 76/min marching through the rhythm. The presence of regular P waves indicates the SA node is functioning normally; the problem is at the AV level.
whyWrongByChoice.E: QRS morphology does provide important information about the level of block in complete heart block: narrow QRS suggests block at or above the His bundle, while wide QRS indicates infra-Hisian block.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a syncopal episode at home. His wife reports that he has had increasing fatigue and dizziness over the past 3 weeks. His medications include metoprolol 50 mg twice daily for hypertension and levothyroxine 75 mcg daily for hypothyroidism. Vital signs reveal a heart rate of 38 bpm and blood pressure of 88/54 mmHg. ECG shows sinus bradycardia with intermittent pauses of 4.2 seconds alternating with bursts of rapid irregular atrial activity. Laboratory studies show a TSH of 0.8 mIU/L. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bradycardia-tachycardia syndrome as a manifestation of sick sinus syndrome

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypothyroidism-related bradycardia due to inadequate levothyroxine dosing

choices.label: C
choices.text: Drug-induced bradycardia from metoprolol requiring dose adjustment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vasovagal syndrome triggered by an identifiable situational stimulus

choices.label: E
choices.text: Physiologic sinus bradycardia associated with high cardiovascular fitness

explanation.summary: The combination of sinus bradycardia with prolonged pauses (>3 seconds) and alternating episodes of rapid atrial activity is characteristic of bradycardia-tachycardia syndrome, a manifestation of sick sinus syndrome (SSS).
explanation.whyCorrect: SSS encompasses sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses, chronotropic incompetence, and bradycardia-tachycardia syndrome. The hallmark finding of sinus pauses greater than 3 seconds combined with alternating tachyarrhythmias (atrial fibrillation) defines brady-tachy syndrome. His age also makes intrinsic SA node degeneration likely.

whyWrongByChoice.C: While metoprolol can cause bradycardia, the presence of prolonged sinus pauses >3 seconds and alternating tachycardia episodes points to intrinsic SA node dysfunction rather than simple drug-induced rate slowing, which would not typically cause alternating tachyarrhythmias.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The TSH of 0.8 mIU/L is within normal range, indicating adequate thyroid hormone replacement. Hypothyroidism-related bradycardia would be expected to show elevated TSH and is typically persistent rather than intermittent with alternating tachycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vasovagal syndrome is episodic and circumstantial, typically triggered by identifiable situations such as micturition, blood draws, or prolonged standing. This patient has progressive symptoms without identifiable triggers and ECG findings of SSS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Physiologic bradycardia in athletes is typically asymptomatic, occurs in physically fit individuals, and demonstrates appropriate heart rate response to exercise. A 72-year-old with syncope and hemodynamic instability does not fit this profile.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with a history of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction is evaluated for progressive exertional dyspnea and recurrent presyncope. ECG reveals sinus bradycardia at 42 bpm with first-degree AV block. Echocardiography shows increased ventricular wall thickness with a granular sparkling appearance, and cardiac MRI demonstrates diffuse late gadolinium enhancement. Technetium pyrophosphate scan is positive. Which of the following is the most likely underlying etiology of her conduction abnormalities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Age-related fibrosis and calcification of the conduction system without infiltrative disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chronic beta-blocker therapy suppressing sinoatrial node automaticity over time

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypothyroidism causing decreased metabolic rate and reduced sympathetic responsiveness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wild-type transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis causing conduction system infiltration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hyperkalemia suppressing sinoatrial node automaticity and slowing myocardial conduction

explanation.summary: The combination of HFpEF, increased ventricular wall thickness with granular sparkling, diffuse late gadolinium enhancement, and a positive technetium pyrophosphate scan is diagnostic of wild-type transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis (ATTRwt), an increasingly recognized cause of conduction system disease in the elderly.
explanation.whyCorrect: ATTRwt is a restrictive cardiomyopathy caused by deposition of misfolded transthyretin protein in the myocardium. It commonly presents with HFpEF and conduction abnormalities including sinus bradycardia. The imaging findings (wall thickening, granular sparkling, LGE, positive pyrophosphate scan) are characteristic of this condition.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While age-related degeneration is the most common cause of SA node dysfunction in the elderly, the imaging findings (granular sparkling, positive pyrophosphate scan) specifically point to an infiltrative process rather than simple degenerative fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypothyroidism is an extrinsic cause of bradycardia that slows heart rate through systemic metabolic effects. It would not explain the characteristic echocardiographic and cardiac MRI findings described, which point to an infiltrative cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Drug-induced bradycardia has a clear temporal relationship with medication use and is a reversible extrinsic cause. The imaging findings indicate structural myocardial disease, not a pharmacologic effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hyperkalemia is an extrinsic factor that can suppress SA node automaticity, but it would not cause the structural cardiac changes seen on echocardiography and MRI. It is diagnosed by serum electrolyte measurement.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with symptomatic sinus bradycardia (heart rate 34 bpm) and blood pressure of 82/50 mmHg is treated with atropine 0.5 mg IV in the emergency department. His heart rate increases to only 40 bpm after two doses. He has a known history of prior inferior myocardial infarction with residual SA node fibrosis. Which of the following best explains the limited efficacy of atropine in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atropine is a beta-adrenergic agonist and requires intact sympathetic nerve terminals to be effective

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atropine primarily increases ventricular contractility rather than chronotropy at the SA node

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atropine acts on nicotinic receptors in the autonomic ganglia and has no direct cardiac effects

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atropine blocks muscarinic receptors to reduce vagal tone but cannot restore automaticity in structurally damaged SA node tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atropine requires concurrent administration of epinephrine to exert any chronotropic effect

explanation.summary: Atropine competitively blocks muscarinic (M2) receptors at the SA node, reducing parasympathetic tone. It is most effective for vagally-mediated bradycardia but has limited efficacy when the underlying cause is intrinsic structural SA node disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: In this patient with SA node fibrosis from prior MI, the bradycardia is due to intrinsic structural damage to pacemaker cells, not excessive vagal tone. Atropine works by blocking parasympathetic input but cannot compensate for the loss of functional pacemaker cells in fibrotic tissue.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atropine is a muscarinic (M2) receptor antagonist, not a beta-adrenergic agonist. It works by blocking parasympathetic input to the SA node, not by stimulating sympathetic pathways.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Atropine does not increase ventricular contractility. Its primary cardiac effect is on heart rate through vagolytic effects at the SA and AV nodes. Increased contractility is mediated by beta-adrenergic stimulation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atropine acts on muscarinic receptors, not nicotinic receptors. It has direct cardiac effects at M2 receptors on the SA and AV nodes, blocking parasympathetic influence on heart rate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atropine can exert its chronotropic effect independently without concurrent epinephrine. While epinephrine or dopamine infusions may be used when atropine is ineffective, they are not required for atropine to function.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old competitive marathon runner presents for a pre-participation sports physical. She is asymptomatic and reports no dizziness, syncope, or exercise intolerance. Resting ECG reveals sinus bradycardia at 48 bpm with normal P-wave morphology and a normal PR interval. She completes a maximal exercise stress test, achieving a peak heart rate of 192 bpm with no arrhythmias. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Initiate ambulatory Holter monitoring to evaluate for occult sinus pauses during sleep

choices.label: B
choices.text: Begin low-dose atropine therapy to maintain a resting heart rate above 60 bpm

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refer for permanent pacemaker implantation to prevent future symptomatic bradycardia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provide reassurance and observation with no further cardiac workup required

choices.label: E
choices.text: Order thyroid function tests and serum electrolytes to exclude pathologic bradycardia

explanation.summary: Athletic bradycardia in young, physically fit individuals is physiologic and typically asymptomatic. With a normal stress test showing appropriate chronotropic response, no treatment or further workup is necessary.
explanation.whyCorrect: Asymptomatic bradycardia in athletes with good cardiovascular fitness is physiologic, resulting from increased vagal tone from conditioning. The appropriate heart rate response to exercise (peak 192 bpm) confirms normal chronotropic competence, ruling out pathologic SA node dysfunction. Reassurance and observation are appropriate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ambulatory monitoring is indicated when there is clinical suspicion for intermittent pathologic bradycardia or correlation between symptoms and bradycardic episodes. In an asymptomatic athlete with normal stress test results, this is unnecessary.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Long-term atropine therapy is impractical and not indicated for physiologic bradycardia. Atropine is reserved for acute symptomatic bradycardia in emergency settings.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Permanent pacemaker implantation is the definitive treatment for symptomatic intrinsic SA node dysfunction. It is not indicated in asymptomatic physiologic bradycardia with normal chronotropic response.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While thyroid function tests and electrolytes are part of the workup for unexplained bradycardia, this patient's clinical picture (young athlete, asymptomatic, normal exercise response) makes pathologic causes very unlikely, and further testing is not warranted.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with sick sinus syndrome and a heart rate of 35 bpm is being monitored in the ICU. His ECG shows absence of P waves with narrow QRS complexes at a regular rate of 45 bpm. Which of the following best describes the mechanism responsible for maintaining his cardiac rhythm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A ventricular escape rhythm originating from the Purkinje fibers with inherently wide QRS complexes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Accelerated junctional rhythm that has usurped the dominant cardiac pacemaker at a rate exceeding 60 bpm

choices.label: C
choices.text: A bundle branch escape rhythm producing narrow complexes due to rapid conduction through the His bundle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response and absent discernible atrial activity

choices.label: E
choices.text: A junctional escape rhythm from the AV node acting as a subsidiary pacemaker due to SA node failure

explanation.summary: When the SA node fails, the fastest available subsidiary pacemaker assumes control. The AV node (junctional pacemaker) fires at 40-60 bpm, producing narrow QRS complexes because impulses conduct normally through the bilateral bundle branches.
explanation.whyCorrect: The absence of P waves with regular narrow QRS complexes at 45 bpm is characteristic of a junctional escape rhythm. When SA node activity ceases, the AV node becomes the dominant pacemaker by default, producing an escape rhythm with a typical rate of 40-60 bpm and narrow QRS complexes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ventricular escape rhythms from the Purkinje fibers would produce wide QRS complexes because depolarization does not follow the normal His-Purkinje conduction pathway. The narrow QRS complexes in this case indicate a supraventricular origin.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Accelerated junctional rhythm has a rate of 60-100 bpm and usurps the dominant rhythm rather than occurring by default. This patient's rate of 45 bpm is within the typical junctional escape range (40-60 bpm), consistent with a true escape rhythm.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Atrial fibrillation would produce an irregularly irregular ventricular response, not the regular rhythm described in this patient. While P waves are absent in both conditions, the regularity of the QRS complexes distinguishes junctional escape from atrial fibrillation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Escape rhythms from the bundle branches would produce wide QRS complexes because only one bundle branch would be generating the impulse, with abnormal ventricular activation. The narrow complexes here indicate the impulse originates above the bifurcation of the His bundle.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department after losing consciousness while urinating. Witnesses report he was pale and diaphoretic before collapsing. In the ED, his heart rate is 52 bpm and blood pressure is 86/58 mmHg. ECG shows sinus bradycardia. No structural heart disease is found on echocardiography. His symptoms have recurred three times in the past year, always associated with micturition. Lifestyle modifications including adequate hydration and situational precautions have been ineffective. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Permanent dual-chamber pacemaker implantation as first-line definitive treatment

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiation of a beta-blocker to prevent reflex tachycardia that triggers subsequent bradycardia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Long-term oral atropine therapy to prevent recurrent vagally-mediated bradycardia episodes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Catheter ablation of the sinoatrial node to eliminate the source of abnormal impulse generation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pharmacotherapy with midodrine or fludrocortisone to address the vasodepressor component

explanation.summary: This patient has vasovagal syndrome (micturition syncope) with both cardioinhibitory (bradycardia) and vasodepressor (hypotension) components. After lifestyle modifications fail, pharmacotherapy with midodrine or fludrocortisone to address the hypotension component is appropriate.
explanation.whyCorrect: Vasovagal episodes manifest as a combination of bradycardia (cardioinhibitory component) and hypotension (vasodepressor component). Management starts with lifestyle interventions; when those fail, medications such as midodrine or fludrocortisone to improve the hypotension component may be helpful. Pacing is reserved as a last resort.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pacemaker implantation is employed as a last resort in vasovagal syndrome and is not indicated in most patients. The vasodepressor (hypotension) component would not be addressed by pacing alone, and pharmacotherapy should be attempted first.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Long-term oral atropine therapy is impractical and not an accepted treatment strategy. Atropine is reserved for acute symptomatic bradycardia in emergency settings and is not suitable for chronic outpatient management.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Catheter ablation of the SA node is not a treatment for vasovagal syndrome. The SA node itself is not dysfunctional; rather, excessive vagal input is causing the bradycardia. Ablation would worsen the condition.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Beta-blockers would exacerbate bradycardia by further suppressing SA node function and are contraindicated in symptomatic bradycardia. They would worsen both the heart rate and blood pressure in this patient.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with chronic heart failure is evaluated for worsening fatigue and exercise intolerance over the past 2 months. She takes digoxin 0.25 mg daily, lisinopril, and furosemide. Resting heart rate is 50 bpm. During an exercise stress test, her heart rate increases from 50 bpm to only 72 bpm at peak exertion, well below the expected age-predicted maximum heart rate. ECG shows sinus rhythm with no pauses. Which of the following best characterizes her condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronotropic incompetence as a component of sick sinus syndrome exacerbated by digoxin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sinus pause with asymptomatic recovery masking underlying SA node arrest on monitoring

choices.label: C
choices.text: Athletic bradycardia from deconditioning-induced enhanced vagal tone in heart failure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypothyroidism-related bradycardia with reduced metabolic rate and blunted exercise response

choices.label: E
choices.text: First-degree AV block causing delayed ventricular activation and reduced exercise capacity

explanation.summary: Chronotropic incompetence is the inability to appropriately increase heart rate with exercise and is a component of sick sinus syndrome. Digoxin, which increases vagal tone and suppresses SA node function, can exacerbate this condition.
explanation.whyCorrect: The failure to achieve an adequate heart rate response during exercise (50 to 72 bpm) despite maximal exertion defines chronotropic incompetence. This is part of the SSS spectrum and is worsened by digoxin, which suppresses SA node function by increasing vagal tone.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Sinus pauses were not observed on the ECG or during stress testing. Chronotropic incompetence is characterized by inadequate heart rate response to exercise, not by intermittent pauses in SA node activity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Athletic bradycardia occurs in physically fit individuals with physiologic enhancement of vagal tone. This patient has heart failure with symptomatic exercise intolerance, not athletic conditioning. Additionally, athletic bradycardia demonstrates appropriate chronotropic response during exercise.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While hypothyroidism can cause bradycardia and reduced exercise capacity, the clinical presentation with heart failure, digoxin use, and inadequate chronotropic response is more consistent with SSS. Thyroid function tests would be needed to evaluate this possibility.
whyWrongByChoice.E: First-degree AV block involves delayed conduction through the AV node (prolonged PR interval) and does not explain the failure of the SA node to appropriately increase its firing rate during exercise.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute symptomatic bradycardia (heart rate 30 bpm) and hemodynamic instability (blood pressure 78/48 mmHg). Atropine 1 mg IV is administered with minimal response. Dopamine infusion is initiated but the patient remains hemodynamically unstable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place a temporary transvenous pacemaker to provide direct myocardial electrical stimulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate intravenous verapamil to improve AV nodal conduction and restore adequate heart rate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer additional atropine at higher doses to overcome parasympathetic tone completely

choices.label: D
choices.text: Begin oral beta-blocker therapy to stabilize the cardiac rhythm through sympathetic modulation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform immediate permanent pacemaker implantation under local anesthesia in the emergency department

explanation.summary: When atropine and chronotropic drug infusions fail to stabilize symptomatic bradycardia, temporary transvenous pacing provides direct myocardial stimulation as a bridge to definitive treatment.
explanation.whyCorrect: Transvenous pacing involves inserting a pacing catheter through a central vein into the right ventricle to deliver electrical impulses directly to the myocardium. It is used for persistent or unstable bradyarrhythmias when medications are ineffective, serving as a bridge to permanent pacemaker implantation.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Atropine has already been administered with minimal response, indicating that the bradycardia is likely not primarily vagally mediated. Additional atropine doses are unlikely to be effective and may cause adverse effects including tachyarrhythmias.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Verapamil is a non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that suppresses SA node automaticity and slows AV conduction. It would worsen the bradycardia and is contraindicated in this setting.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-blockers reduce heart rate by blocking sympathetic stimulation at beta-1 receptors and are contraindicated in symptomatic bradycardia. They would further suppress SA node function and worsen hemodynamic instability.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Permanent pacemaker implantation is the definitive treatment but requires a planned procedure in an appropriate setting, not emergency implantation. Temporary pacing bridges the patient to this definitive therapy.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is found unresponsive after a severe traumatic brain injury. In the ICU, her heart rate is 42 bpm and blood pressure is 168/102 mmHg. CT scan shows diffuse cerebral edema with evidence of elevated intracranial pressure. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of her bradycardia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct myocardial contusion from chest trauma causing intrinsic SA node dysfunction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hyperkalemia from tissue injury suppressing SA node automaticity and conduction velocity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypothermia-induced slowing of all metabolic processes including SA node automaticity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stimulation of the vagus nerve as part of the Cushing reflex in response to elevated intracranial pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reduced sympathetic outflow due to cervical spinal cord injury causing neurogenic bradycardia

explanation.summary: Increased intracranial pressure from any cause can stimulate the vagus nerve, leading to bradycardia as part of the Cushing reflex (Cushing triad: hypertension, bradycardia, and irregular respirations).
explanation.whyCorrect: The Cushing reflex is a physiologic response to elevated intracranial pressure in which vagal stimulation produces bradycardia, while sympathetic activation causes hypertension. This patient's combination of bradycardia and hypertension following traumatic brain injury with cerebral edema is classic for this reflex.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The clinical scenario describes a traumatic brain injury, not chest trauma. There is no mention of thoracic injury. The combination of bradycardia with hypertension after brain injury points to a central nervous system mechanism, not direct cardiac damage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no mention of hypothermia in this patient. While hypothermia slows SA node automaticity, the hypertension present in this case is more consistent with the Cushing reflex than with hypothermia, which would typically cause hypotension.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While hyperkalemia from rhabdomyolysis could theoretically occur after trauma, it would suppress SA node automaticity without causing the hypertension seen here. The combination of bradycardia and hypertension is characteristic of the Cushing reflex.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cervical spinal cord injury causes neurogenic shock with bradycardia and hypotension due to loss of sympathetic tone. This patient has hypertension, not hypotension, making the Cushing reflex the more likely mechanism.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with recently diagnosed atrial fibrillation is started on diltiazem for rate control. Two weeks later, he presents with progressive fatigue and lightheadedness. His heart rate is 44 bpm and ECG shows sinus bradycardia. The diltiazem is discontinued, and 48 hours later, his heart rate normalizes to 72 bpm with resolution of symptoms. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which diltiazem caused bradycardia in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blocking beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the SA node, reducing sympathetic-mediated chronotropy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enhancing vagal tone through stimulation of muscarinic receptors on the SA node pacemaker cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reducing thyroid hormone synthesis peripherally, thereby decreasing metabolic rate and cardiac chronotropy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibiting sodium-potassium ATPase in cardiac myocytes, leading to increased intracellular calcium concentrations

choices.label: E
choices.text: Directly suppressing SA node automaticity by blocking calcium channels essential for pacemaker cell function

explanation.summary: Diltiazem is a non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that directly suppresses SA node automaticity by blocking calcium channels essential for pacemaker cell function, leading to bradycardia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers like diltiazem and verapamil directly suppress SA node automaticity by blocking L-type calcium channels that are essential for the phase 4 depolarization of pacemaker cells. This is a common and reversible cause of drug-induced sinus bradycardia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Blocking beta-1 adrenergic receptors is the mechanism of beta-blockers (e.g., metoprolol, atenolol), not calcium channel blockers like diltiazem. These are distinct drug classes with different pharmacologic targets.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Enhancing vagal tone through muscarinic receptor stimulation is the mechanism by which digoxin causes bradycardia. Diltiazem acts by directly blocking calcium channels, not by modulating vagal tone.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhibiting sodium-potassium ATPase describes the mechanism of digoxin (cardiac glycosides), not calcium channel blockers. This mechanism leads to increased intracellular calcium and enhanced contractility, not direct suppression of SA node automaticity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diltiazem does not affect thyroid hormone synthesis. Reduced thyroid function causing bradycardia is associated with hypothyroidism or antithyroid medications, not calcium channel blockers.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with known sick sinus syndrome and a permanent pacemaker presents for a routine follow-up. He reports excellent exercise tolerance and no symptoms. His pacemaker interrogation reveals that the device adjusts the pacing rate from 60 bpm at rest to 110 bpm during his daily walks. Which of the following pacemaker features is responsible for this physiologic heart rate adjustment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fixed-rate pacing mode that delivers impulses at a constant predetermined rate regardless of activity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rate-responsive pacing that adjusts heart rate based on the patient's physiological demands

choices.label: C
choices.text: Overdrive pacing designed to suppress competing intrinsic atrial tachyarrhythmias

choices.label: D
choices.text: Demand pacing that only fires when the intrinsic heart rate falls below a set minimum threshold

choices.label: E
choices.text: Antitachycardia pacing that delivers rapid bursts to terminate reentrant supraventricular circuits

explanation.summary: Modern pacemakers can provide rate-responsive pacing that adjusts heart rate based on physiological demands, using sensors that detect activity level, minute ventilation, or other physiologic parameters.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rate-responsive pacing uses activity sensors (accelerometers, minute ventilation sensors) to detect the patient's level of physical activity and adjust the pacing rate accordingly. This mimics the normal physiologic increase in heart rate during exercise, which is particularly important in patients with chronotropic incompetence from SSS.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fixed-rate pacing delivers impulses at a constant rate and does not adjust to physiologic demands. This would not explain the rate increase from 60 to 110 bpm during exercise.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Overdrive pacing is used to suppress tachyarrhythmias by pacing at a rate faster than the arrhythmia. This patient's rate adjustment occurs during normal exercise, not in response to an arrhythmia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Demand pacing senses intrinsic cardiac activity and only delivers a pacing impulse when the heart rate falls below a set threshold. While this prevents competition with intrinsic rhythm, it does not increase the rate during exercise.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Antitachycardia pacing delivers rapid burst pacing to terminate reentrant tachycardias. This is not the mechanism for physiologic rate adjustment during exercise in a patient with SSS.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents to the ED with severe dizziness. His heart rate is 32 bpm with blood pressure of 80/50 mmHg. ECG shows sinus bradycardia. Atropine 0.5 mg IV is ineffective. While awaiting transvenous pacemaker placement, the team decides to attempt transcutaneous pacing. Large pacing pads are placed on his chest and electrical impulses are delivered. Which of the following is the primary limitation of transcutaneous pacing in this clinical setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It can only pace the atria and has no effect on ventricular depolarization or contraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: It requires surgical implantation of leads and therefore cannot be performed emergently

choices.label: C
choices.text: It delivers unsynchronized shocks that carry a high risk of inducing ventricular fibrillation

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is difficult to implement effectively and lacks strong evidence for improved patient outcomes

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is contraindicated in patients with intrinsic SA node disease and only works for vagal bradycardia

explanation.summary: While transcutaneous pacing can provide temporary heart rate support in emergency situations, it is difficult to implement effectively and lacks good evidence for improved patient outcomes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Transcutaneous pacing delivers electrical impulses through the skin to stimulate ventricular depolarization. However, it is technically challenging to implement reliably, is often painful for the patient, and evidence for improved clinical outcomes is limited. It serves primarily as a bridge to more definitive therapy such as transvenous pacing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Transcutaneous pacing stimulates ventricular depolarization and contraction through large pacing pads placed on the chest. The electrical impulses pass through the thoracic wall to depolarize the ventricles.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Transcutaneous pacing is a noninvasive method that does not require surgical lead implantation. It uses external pads placed on the chest and can be initiated rapidly in emergency situations.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Transcutaneous pacing delivers pacing impulses, not unsynchronized shocks. The pacing impulses are delivered at a set rate to stimulate ventricular contraction, which is different from defibrillation or unsynchronized cardioversion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transcutaneous pacing is not contraindicated in intrinsic SA node disease. It can be used for any cause of symptomatic bradycardia as a temporary measure while preparing for more definitive treatment.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute onset palpitations and dizziness lasting 20 minutes. She has no cardiac history. Her heart rate is 178 bpm and blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg. ECG reveals a regular narrow QRS complex tachycardia with no clearly discernible P waves. Carotid sinus massage is performed, and the tachycardia abruptly terminates, restoring sinus rhythm at 78 bpm. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrioventricular nodal reentrant tachycardia sustained by dual AV nodal pathway micro-reentry

choices.label: B
choices.text: Multifocal atrial tachycardia arising from multiple ectopic atrial foci with variable morphology

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction causing a regular tachycardia with hidden flutter waves

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia with gradual onset in response to physiologic stress or underlying illness

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response and irregularly irregular rhythm pattern

explanation.summary: AVNRT is characterized by abrupt onset and termination, regular narrow QRS tachycardia, absent or retrograde P waves, and responsiveness to vagal maneuvers. It is the most common SVT in young people and often occurs without structural heart disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: The regular narrow complex tachycardia with abrupt onset and termination by carotid sinus massage (a vagal maneuver) is classic for AVNRT. Vagal maneuvers slow AV nodal conduction and can terminate AVNRT in approximately 25% of cases by disrupting the reentry circuit within the AV node.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction would typically present at a ventricular rate of approximately 150 bpm and would show a sawtooth pattern of flutter waves. Vagal maneuvers may slow the ventricular rate in atrial flutter but typically do not terminate the arrhythmia, as the sustaining mechanism lies in the atria.
whyWrongByChoice.B: MAT produces an irregular tachycardia with at least 3 different P-wave morphologies and is most commonly associated with pulmonary disease. It would not present as a regular rhythm and would not be terminated by vagal maneuvers.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sinus tachycardia has gradual onset and resolution, not the abrupt onset and termination seen here. It also shows normal P waves preceding each QRS complex and is a physiologic response, not an arrhythmia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atrial fibrillation produces an irregularly irregular rhythm, not the regular tachycardia described. Additionally, vagal maneuvers would slow the ventricular rate in AF but would not terminate the atrial fibrillation itself.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with COPD presents with worsening dyspnea during an acute exacerbation. His heart rate is 118 bpm and irregular. ECG shows a narrow QRS complex tachycardia with at least four distinctly different P-wave morphologies and varying P-P intervals. Serum potassium is 3.1 mEq/L and magnesium is 1.4 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most important initial management strategy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate synchronized cardioversion to restore normal sinus rhythm before further evaluation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administration of adenosine 6 mg IV push to terminate the underlying reentrant tachyarrhythmia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Treatment of the underlying pulmonary disease and correction of electrolyte disturbances

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initiation of amiodarone infusion to achieve rhythm control and prevent recurrent arrhythmia episodes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Catheter ablation of the multiple ectopic atrial foci to permanently eliminate the arrhythmia substrate

explanation.summary: The ECG findings of irregular tachycardia with at least 3 different P-wave morphologies in a patient with COPD are diagnostic of multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT). The primary management is treating the underlying pulmonary disease and correcting electrolyte abnormalities.
explanation.whyCorrect: MAT occurs most commonly during pulmonary disease exacerbations and is associated with hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia. In most cases, treating the underlying COPD exacerbation and correcting electrolyte disturbances (potassium and magnesium) will restore normal sinus rhythm without specific antiarrhythmic therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Synchronized cardioversion is not the appropriate initial management for MAT. Because MAT arises from multiple ectopic foci rather than a single reentry circuit, cardioversion is unlikely to be effective and does not address the underlying cause.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Adenosine is effective for terminating reentrant tachycardias like AVNRT but is not appropriate for MAT, which originates from multiple atrial foci rather than a reentry circuit. Adenosine would not eliminate the multiple ectopic sources.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Amiodarone is not the first-line treatment for MAT. The priority is addressing the reversible underlying causes (pulmonary disease, electrolyte abnormalities). Rate control with beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers may be used if needed.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Catheter ablation is not feasible or indicated for MAT because there are multiple ectopic foci throughout the atria. The arrhythmia is typically self-limiting once the underlying pulmonary disease and electrolyte abnormalities are corrected.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man undergoes a routine pre-employment ECG. He is completely asymptomatic with no cardiac history. The ECG during normal sinus rhythm reveals a PR interval of 110 msec, a slurred upstroke on the initial portion of the QRS complex, and mild QTc prolongation. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these ECG findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: First-degree AV block with delayed conduction through a diseased AV node causing PR prolongation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome with ventricular pre-excitation via an accessory pathway (Kent bundle)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Left bundle branch block causing delayed depolarization of the left ventricle and widened QRS complex

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with septal hypertrophy causing Q waves in lateral leads on the ECG

choices.label: E
choices.text: Brugada syndrome with characteristic ST-segment elevation in the right precordial leads of the ECG

explanation.summary: The clinical triad of shortened PR interval (<120 msec), delta wave (slurred upstroke of the QRS), and QTc prolongation is characteristic of Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome, caused by an accessory pathway (Kent bundle) that bypasses the AV node.
explanation.whyCorrect: WPW syndrome produces a characteristic triad on baseline ECG: shortened PR interval (<120 msec) because the impulse bypasses the normal AV nodal delay through the accessory pathway, a delta wave (slurred upstroke of the R wave) from pre-excitation of ventricular muscle by the accessory pathway, and QTc prolongation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: First-degree AV block is characterized by a prolonged PR interval (>200 msec), which is the opposite of the shortened PR interval (<120 msec) seen in WPW. It does not produce delta waves.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Left bundle branch block produces a wide QRS complex with specific morphologic changes but does not cause a shortened PR interval or delta wave. The slurred upstroke in LBBB occurs later in the QRS, unlike the initial delta wave of WPW.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy may produce abnormal Q waves and increased QRS voltage but does not cause a shortened PR interval or the characteristic delta wave pattern seen in WPW.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Brugada syndrome shows characteristic ST elevation in leads V1-V3 with a specific morphology but does not produce a shortened PR interval or delta wave. The ECG findings described are specific to WPW.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital with new-onset heart failure. Her heart rate is 150 bpm and regular. ECG shows a narrow QRS tachycardia with a sawtooth pattern of atrial activity best seen in leads II, III, and aVF. The calculated atrial rate is approximately 300 bpm. Which of the following conduction ratios best explains the relationship between the atrial and ventricular rates in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1:1 conduction with the AV node transmitting every atrial impulse to the ventricles without delay

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2:1 conduction with the AV node blocking every other atrial impulse due to its refractory period

choices.label: C
choices.text: 3:1 conduction with the AV node transmitting only one of every three flutter waves to the ventricles

choices.label: D
choices.text: 4:1 conduction resulting in a ventricular rate of approximately 75 beats per minute at rest

choices.label: E
choices.text: Variable conduction with irregular transmission of flutter waves producing an irregularly irregular rhythm

explanation.summary: In typical atrial flutter with an atrial rate of ~300 bpm, the AV node cannot conduct every impulse due to its inherent refractory period. The most common conduction ratio is 2:1, resulting in a ventricular rate of ~150 bpm.
explanation.whyCorrect: The AV node has inherently slow conduction and a filtering function that prevents all 300 atrial impulses per minute from reaching the ventricles. The typical conduction ratio is 2:1 (300/2 = 150 bpm), which matches this patient's ventricular rate of 150 bpm.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 1:1 conduction would produce a ventricular rate of 300 bpm, which is not possible under normal AV nodal physiology. The AV node's refractory period prevents conduction of every impulse at this rapid atrial rate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 3:1 conduction would produce a ventricular rate of 100 bpm (300/3), which does not match the patient's ventricular rate of 150 bpm. While 3:1 conduction can occur, it is less common than 2:1.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 4:1 conduction would yield a ventricular rate of 75 bpm (300/4), which is significantly lower than this patient's rate of 150 bpm. This ratio occurs but is not consistent with the clinical findings.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Variable conduction would produce an irregular ventricular rhythm with varying R-R intervals. This patient has a regular ventricular rate of 150 bpm, indicating a fixed 2:1 conduction ratio.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome presents with recurrent episodes of palpitations occurring 2-3 times per month. He has been managed with acute adenosine for symptomatic episodes. He now requests a more definitive treatment option to prevent future episodes. Which of the following is the most appropriate long-term management strategy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lifelong oral adenosine therapy taken daily to prevent tachyarrhythmia recurrence and maintain sinus rhythm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Permanent pacemaker implantation to override the accessory pathway and prevent reentrant circuits

choices.label: C
choices.text: Surgical maze procedure to create scar tissue barriers preventing atrial macro-reentrant circuits

choices.label: D
choices.text: Catheter radioablation of the accessory pathway performed in the electrophysiology laboratory

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chronic anticoagulation with warfarin to prevent thromboembolic complications from the arrhythmia

explanation.summary: Catheter radioablation of the abnormal accessory pathway (Kent bundle) in the electrophysiology lab is the first-line definitive treatment for WPW syndrome with recurrent symptomatic tachycardia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Once the patient is not acutely tachycardic, the first-line curative treatment for WPW is catheter radioablation of the abnormal pathway (Kent bundle) performed in the electrophysiology laboratory. This eliminates the substrate for the reentrant tachycardia and can be potentially curative.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenosine is an IV medication used for acute termination of SVT episodes and is not available or appropriate as a chronic oral therapy. It has an extremely short half-life and is only used in acute settings.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pacemaker implantation is used for bradyarrhythmias such as sick sinus syndrome, not for WPW. The problem in WPW is an accessory conduction pathway, not a failure of impulse generation that would require pacing.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The surgical maze procedure is used for atrial fibrillation, not WPW. It creates barriers to interrupt macro-reentrant circuits in the atria, whereas WPW requires targeted ablation of the specific accessory pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chronic anticoagulation is not standard therapy for WPW syndrome. Anticoagulation is indicated for atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter to prevent stroke, but WPW with AVRT does not carry the same thromboembolic risk.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman with recurrent AVNRT is brought to the emergency department during an acute episode. Her heart rate is 188 bpm and she is hemodynamically stable with blood pressure of 110/68 mmHg. Valsalva maneuver is attempted but fails to terminate the arrhythmia. Which of the following medications is the most appropriate next step, with an efficacy rate exceeding 90% for termination of this arrhythmia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adenosine administered as a rapid intravenous push to transiently block AV nodal conduction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amlodipine administered orally to reduce calcium-dependent conduction through the AV node

choices.label: C
choices.text: Amiodarone administered as a slow intravenous infusion to suppress abnormal atrial automaticity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Digoxin administered intravenously to increase vagal tone and slow AV nodal conduction velocity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atropine administered intravenously to block parasympathetic input and accelerate AV nodal conduction

explanation.summary: Adenosine is effective in more than 90% of AVNRT cases and is the first-line medication after vagal maneuvers fail in stable patients with AVNRT.
explanation.whyCorrect: Adenosine is administered as a rapid IV push and transiently blocks AV nodal conduction, interrupting the reentrant circuit that sustains AVNRT. It is effective in more than 90% of cases and has a very short half-life, limiting the duration of any adverse effects.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Amlodipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that has little or no effect on the AV node at standard doses. Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem) are effective for AVNRT, but amlodipine is not.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Amiodarone is a broad-spectrum antiarrhythmic but is not first-line for acute AVNRT termination. It has a slow onset of action and significant side effects. Adenosine is preferred for its rapid efficacy and short duration of action.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Digoxin increases vagal tone and can slow AV nodal conduction, but its onset of action is too slow for acute termination of AVNRT. It is not appropriate as acute therapy when rapid termination is needed.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atropine blocks parasympathetic input and would accelerate AV nodal conduction, potentially worsening the tachycardia. It is used for bradycardia, not tachyarrhythmias. It would facilitate rather than interrupt the reentrant circuit.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with chronic heart failure presents with palpitations and dyspnea. ECG shows a regular atrial rhythm at 160 bpm with discrete abnormal P waves and 1:1 AV conduction producing a ventricular rate of 160 bpm. He takes digoxin 0.25 mg daily. Serum digoxin level returns elevated at 3.2 ng/mL. Which of the following ECG findings would most strongly suggest digitalis toxicity as the cause of this arrhythmia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Presence of sawtooth flutter waves at 300 bpm with regular 2:1 conduction to the ventricles

choices.label: B
choices.text: Irregularly irregular rhythm with absence of organized atrial activity and fibrillatory baseline

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atrial tachycardia with 2:1 or higher conduction ratios rather than the typical 1:1 conduction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Regular narrow complex tachycardia at 190 bpm with retrograde P waves buried in the QRS

choices.label: E
choices.text: Multiple different P-wave morphologies with irregular P-P intervals and rate above 100 bpm

explanation.summary: Digitalis intoxication characteristically causes atrial tachycardia with conduction ratios other than 1:1 (e.g., 2:1 or higher). Digoxin enhances vagal tone, so when atrial tachycardia is caused by digitalis, higher conduction ratios are seen, which are unusual with other causes.
explanation.whyCorrect: When atrial tachycardia is caused by digitalis preparations, 2:1 or higher conduction ratios are seen due to digoxin's enhancement of vagal effects on the AV node. This combination (atrial tachycardia with block) is unusual with other causes and should immediately raise suspicion for digitalis toxicity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sawtooth flutter waves at 300 bpm with 2:1 conduction describe typical atrial flutter, which is caused by a macro-reentrant circuit in the right atrium. While digitalis can contribute to atrial flutter, the sawtooth pattern itself is not specific for digitalis toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An irregularly irregular rhythm with no organized atrial activity describes atrial fibrillation, which has many causes and is not specifically diagnostic of digitalis toxicity. The ECG pattern is distinct from atrial tachycardia with block.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Regular narrow complex tachycardia with retrograde P waves describes AVNRT, which is caused by dual AV nodal pathway micro-reentry. This is not characteristically associated with digitalis toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Multiple P-wave morphologies with irregular intervals describe multifocal atrial tachycardia, which is most commonly associated with pulmonary disease. While digitalis toxicity is listed as a possible cause of MAT, the more characteristic finding is atrial tachycardia with conduction block.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with palpitations and mild dizziness. ECG shows atrial flutter with a ventricular rate of 142 bpm. She is hemodynamically stable. Rate control is achieved with IV diltiazem, reducing the ventricular rate to 78 bpm. However, the flutter waves persist on ECG. Her CHA2DS2-VASc score is 3. Which of the following additional management considerations is most important for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No anticoagulation is needed because atrial flutter carries negligible stroke risk compared to atrial fibrillation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aspirin alone is sufficient for stroke prevention in atrial flutter regardless of CHA2DS2-VASc score

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anticoagulation should be initiated because atrial flutter carries a stroke risk similar to atrial fibrillation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anticoagulation is only indicated if the atrial flutter converts to atrial fibrillation on subsequent monitoring

choices.label: E
choices.text: Anticoagulation can be safely deferred until catheter ablation of the flutter circuit is performed electively

explanation.summary: Current guidelines indicate that atrial flutter and atrial fibrillation should be treated the same when it comes to stroke prevention with anticoagulation. Atrial flutter carries a stroke risk similar to atrial fibrillation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atrial flutter carries a stroke risk that is similar, though slightly lower in some reports, to atrial fibrillation. Current guidelines mandate that atrial flutter and fibrillation should be treated the same regarding stroke prevention with anticoagulation, especially in patients with elevated CHA2DS2-VASc scores.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This is incorrect. Atrial flutter does carry significant stroke risk, and current guidelines recommend treating it the same as atrial fibrillation for anticoagulation purposes. Failure to anticoagulate exposes the patient to preventable thromboembolic complications.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Aspirin alone is insufficient for stroke prevention in atrial flutter with elevated CHA2DS2-VASc scores. Full anticoagulation (with direct oral anticoagulants or warfarin) is recommended, similar to the approach used for atrial fibrillation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anticoagulation should not be withheld pending conversion to atrial fibrillation. Atrial flutter independently carries significant stroke risk, and guidelines recommend anticoagulation based on the CHA2DS2-VASc score regardless of whether atrial fibrillation coexists.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anticoagulation should not be deferred while waiting for ablation. The stroke risk exists as long as the arrhythmia is present, and anticoagulation should be initiated promptly based on the patient's risk factors.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man with Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome presents with a regular narrow complex tachycardia at 184 bpm. He is hemodynamically stable. Adenosine 6 mg IV push is administered and successfully terminates the tachycardia. Post-conversion ECG shows sinus rhythm with a short PR interval and delta waves. Which of the following best explains why the QRS complex during the tachycardia was narrow despite the presence of an accessory pathway?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The accessory pathway was used for retrograde conduction only while antegrade conduction proceeded normally through the AV node

choices.label: B
choices.text: The accessory pathway was completely blocked during the tachycardia, preventing any pre-excitation from occurring

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tachycardia rate was too fast for the accessory pathway to conduct, rendering it functionally inactive

choices.label: D
choices.text: The delta wave and pre-excitation only appear during bradycardic states and are suppressed by tachycardia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Adenosine eliminated the accessory pathway before conversion, so the narrow QRS reflected pathway ablation

explanation.summary: In orthodromic AVRT, the reentrant impulse travels antegradely down the AV junction and bundle branches (producing a narrow QRS) and retrogradely through the accessory pathway back to the atria, completing the macro-reentrant circuit.
explanation.whyCorrect: During orthodromic AVRT in WPW, the circuit involves antegrade conduction through the normal AV node/His-Purkinje pathway (producing a narrow, normal-appearing QRS) and retrograde conduction through the accessory pathway (Kent bundle) back to the atria. Since ventricular activation occurs via the normal conduction system, the QRS remains narrow.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The accessory pathway is actively participating in the tachycardia circuit; it is not blocked. It conducts retrogradely, carrying the impulse from the ventricles back to the atria to complete the reentrant loop.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The accessory pathway is functional during the tachycardia and is essential to maintaining the reentrant circuit. The rate does not prevent accessory pathway conduction; rather, the pathway conducts in the retrograde direction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Delta waves and pre-excitation on baseline ECG reflect antegrade conduction through the accessory pathway during sinus rhythm. During the tachycardia, the pathway conducts retrogradely only, so pre-excitation is not seen. This is not related to heart rate suppression.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Adenosine has a transient effect lasting only seconds and does not ablate or permanently alter the accessory pathway. The pathway remains intact, as evidenced by the delta waves seen on the post-conversion sinus rhythm ECG.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a heart rate of 196 bpm and blood pressure of 72/44 mmHg. She is diaphoretic and confused. ECG shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia. Vagal maneuvers and adenosine administration fail to terminate the arrhythmia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer oral verapamil and monitor for conversion to sinus rhythm over the next several hours

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate intravenous amiodarone loading dose for chemical cardioversion of the reentrant tachycardia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perform synchronized cardioversion with the shock timed to the R wave to avoid the vulnerable period

choices.label: D
choices.text: Attempt repeat carotid sinus massage with ice water immersion to maximize parasympathetic vagal tone

choices.label: E
choices.text: Place a temporary transvenous pacemaker to overdrive pace and terminate the tachyarrhythmia circuit

explanation.summary: In an unstable patient with SVT who does not respond to vagal maneuvers or adenosine, synchronized cardioversion is the appropriate next step. The shock is synchronized to the R wave to avoid the vulnerable repolarization period.
explanation.whyCorrect: Synchronized cardioversion delivers a timed electrical shock synchronized with the R wave of the ECG to terminate unstable tachyarrhythmias. This timing avoids delivery during the vulnerable repolarization phase, reducing the risk of inducing ventricular fibrillation. It is indicated when the patient is hemodynamically unstable and pharmacologic measures have failed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Oral verapamil has a slow onset and is inappropriate for an acutely unstable patient with hypotension and altered mental status. Hemodynamic instability requires immediate intervention, not expectant management with oral medications.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While amiodarone can be used for certain arrhythmias, it has a slow onset of action and is not the preferred approach for an unstable patient who has already failed first-line pharmacotherapy. Synchronized cardioversion provides rapid and definitive termination.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vagal maneuvers have already been attempted and failed. Repeating them in a hemodynamically unstable patient delays definitive treatment. The priority is immediate cardioversion to restore hemodynamic stability.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Overdrive pacing is not the standard approach for acute SVT in an unstable patient. Synchronized cardioversion is more immediately available and effective for terminating the tachyarrhythmia in this emergency setting.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with palpitations. ECG shows a tachycardia at 162 bpm with a regular, narrow QRS complex. His physician is trying to distinguish between sinus tachycardia and a paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia. Which of the following clinical features most reliably differentiates sinus tachycardia from PSVT?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia always presents with a heart rate above 150 bpm while PSVT is typically below 150 bpm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia has gradual onset and resolution while PSVT has characteristically abrupt onset and termination

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia produces wide QRS complexes while PSVT uniformly produces narrow QRS complexes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia is always irregular while PSVT is always perfectly regular without rate variation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia responds to adenosine with permanent termination while PSVT is resistant to adenosine

explanation.summary: The key distinguishing feature between sinus tachycardia and PSVT is the pattern of onset and termination. Sinus tachycardia has gradual onset and resolution, while PSVT (including AVNRT, AVRT) has abrupt onset and termination.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sinus tachycardia is a physiologic increase in sinus rate in response to activity, stress, or illness and thus demonstrates gradual onset and resolution. In contrast, PSVT is defined as a regular narrow complex tachycardia of abrupt onset and termination, which is one of its most reliable distinguishing features.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Both sinus tachycardia and PSVT can present at rates above or below 150 bpm. PSVT typically has a rate range of 110-200 bpm, and sinus tachycardia can also exceed 150 bpm. Heart rate alone does not reliably distinguish them.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Both sinus tachycardia and PSVT typically produce narrow QRS complexes because impulses are conducted through the normal His-Purkinje system. Wide QRS can occur in either if bundle branch block is present.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sinus tachycardia is regular (with mild sinus arrhythmia), and PSVT is also regular. Irregularity is not a distinguishing feature between these two rhythms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This is the opposite of the true relationship. Adenosine terminates PSVT (especially AVNRT) in more than 90% of cases but does not terminate sinus tachycardia, which will transiently slow and then resume.

questions.stem: A 31-year-old woman with no known cardiac history presents with intermittent palpitations described as a sensation of her heart 'skipping a beat.' She reports the episodes are brief and self-resolving. She drinks 4 cups of coffee daily and has been under significant work stress. ECG shows normal sinus rhythm with an occasional early beat preceded by an abnormal P wave followed by a normal QRS complex. Echocardiography is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Initiate daily beta-blocker therapy to suppress the ectopic atrial focus and prevent recurrence

choices.label: B
choices.text: Refer for catheter ablation of the ectopic focus to definitively eliminate the source of the arrhythmia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Start oral flecainide to prevent progression from premature atrial contractions to atrial fibrillation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Begin anticoagulation therapy due to the risk of atrial thrombus formation from irregular contractions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reassure the patient that no specific treatment is required as PACs are generally benign

explanation.summary: Premature atrial contractions are the least dangerous supraventricular arrhythmia. In a patient with a structurally normal heart, PACs generally require no treatment, and reassurance is appropriate.
explanation.whyCorrect: PACs are common, minimally symptomatic arrhythmias that generally require no treatment. They arise from an ectopic focus in the atria and produce an abnormal P wave followed by a normal QRS. In patients with normal echocardiography and no structural heart disease, they are benign and reassurance is the appropriate management.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Beta-blocker therapy is not indicated for occasional, benign PACs in a patient with no structural heart disease. The potential side effects of daily medication outweigh any benefit for this benign condition.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Catheter ablation is reserved for symptomatic, refractory arrhythmias and is not appropriate for occasional PACs. The invasive nature of the procedure is not justified for this benign finding.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Flecainide is an antiarrhythmic agent used for more serious arrhythmias and carries proarrhythmic risks. It is not indicated for benign PACs, and there is no established progression from PACs to atrial fibrillation requiring prophylaxis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anticoagulation is not indicated for PACs. The thromboembolic risk associated with atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter does not apply to isolated premature atrial contractions, which do not cause sustained atrial stasis.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old medical student with no prior cardiac history experiences sudden onset of rapid palpitations during an exam. Her heart rate is 172 bpm with a regular narrow QRS complex tachycardia on ECG. She is hemodynamically stable. Her physician suspects AVNRT based on the clinical presentation. Which of the following best describes the underlying anatomic substrate that predisposes this patient to AVNRT?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A congenital accessory pathway (Kent bundle) connecting the atria to the ventricles and bypassing the AV node

choices.label: B
choices.text: Structural remodeling of the atrial myocardium with fibrosis creating a macro-reentrant circuit in the right atrium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dual AV nodal pathways with fast and slow conduction properties, an anatomic variant present in 7-10% of the population

choices.label: D
choices.text: Multiple ectopic atrial foci with enhanced automaticity triggered by elevated catecholamine levels during stress

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acquired AV nodal disease from prior myocarditis causing areas of slow conduction and unidirectional block

explanation.summary: AVNRT is caused by micro-reentry within the AV node involving dual AV nodal pathways (fast and slow pathways). This anatomic variation is present in 7-10% of the population, with a prevalence roughly equal to being left-handed.
explanation.whyCorrect: The substrate for AVNRT is the presence of dual AV nodal pathways with different conduction velocities and refractory periods. This is not considered pathological but rather an anatomic variation, existing in 7-10% of the population. It is the most common SVT in young people and often occurs without structural heart disease.
